Spec does not specify the analysis scope: per-module, per-crate, per-binary, post-link, or link-time (LTO-equivalent)? How do public signatures at module boundaries interact — an existential returned from a 'pub fn' could be packed by any future consumer. How do §14 contracts narrow the world (a decoder produces exactly the type declared in the contract)? How does §25.6 profile-based restriction narrow the world (an embedded profile transitively excludes modules that require heap, shrinking the candidate packer set)? What about 'extern "C"' boundaries where the compiler cannot see the packing site? What about dynamically-loaded packages (out of scope for FX given §25)? The answer matters because it determines how aggressively the compiler can devirtualize and how much of §21 optimization survives into real binaries.
gaps.prior_art: Rust's devirtualization is crate-local by default (LLVM sees only one crate at a time); 'rustc -C lto' enables cross-crate. GHC whole-program via MLton/Scala Native/PureScript is global. Swift's resilience boundaries (SIL 'emit-into-client' vs opaque) control which optimizations cross module boundaries per published ABI stability rules. Java HotSpot uses dynamic CHA refined at runtime with deoptimization fallback. C++ LTO + /OPT:ICF + devirtualization in MSVC or clang -flto=thin.

gaps.name: pattern_match_emission_strategy
gaps.description: §4.3 specifies pattern match semantics (exhaustiveness, ownership flow, refinement propagation) but not emission strategy. 'match x;' where 'x: color' (3-variant enum) is typically lowered to a 3-way branch chain; for large enums with dense tags 0..N a jump table is optimal; for constructor destructuring with nested patterns a decision tree is optimal; for 2-arm Bool/Option match producing primitives cmov is optimal. Spec silent on which. Matters because: (a) verified compilation (§20.8) requires specified output, (b) CT contexts ban some branch-based tag dispatch (gap #142), (c) performance is predictable only if emission rule is published. Also relevant: machine transition dispatch (§13) and actor receive (§13.14) are 1-D and 2-D variants of the same problem.
gaps.prior_art: LLVM's switch lowering pass uses density + size heuristics (jump table when dense and ≥ 4 cases; binary search for sparse dense clusters; branch chain for small). GCC's switchconv pass. OCaml's decision-tree generation per Maranget 'Compiling Pattern Matching to Good Decision Trees' (ML 2008). Rust MIR pattern match lowering uses Maranget with integer-switch optimization. Scala 3 match tree optimization per Sinot-Maranget. Haskell's case elaboration into STG / pattern-match compilation.

gaps.name: constant_time_dispatch_emission
gaps.description: §12.5 forbids branching on secret values in CT contexts — but pattern match IS a branch and existential call through a fn pointer IS an indirect branch (with different targets potentially having different instruction sequences and timings). Spec doesn't say which dispatch forms survive under 'with CT': (a) pattern match on non-secret tag where arms have different timing — does this leak the tag? or only if the arm's output is secret-dependent? (b) existential call where different concrete targets have different internal timing — leaks the packed type? (c) closed-enum match on non-secret tag with all arms emitting CT-equivalent code — safe. The speculative-devirt guard of gap #139 ('if fn_ptr == expected') is particularly dangerous in CT: the branch tests fn pointer identity, leaking the concrete type.
gaps.prior_art: CT-wasm (Watt et al. POPL 2019) proves constant-time by forbidding secret-dependent control flow entirely. FaCT (Cauligi et al. PLDI 2019) transforms secret-dependent branches into cmov sequences. Jasmin (used by Libjade) requires systematic protection + CT region + no indirect calls whose target differs based on secret data. BoringSSL uses 'ct_select' for branch-free dispatch. 'Constant-Time Foundations for the New Spectre Era' (von Gleissenthall et al. CCS 2020) formalizes the model including indirect branches. CT-Wasmtime (arXiv:2311.14246) achieves end-to-end verified CT compilation but only for synchronous code with uniform dispatch.

gaps.name: dispatch_hints_likely_cold
gaps.description: No attribute in §17.4 for biasing branch prediction / emit order. Modern compilers support likely / unlikely on match arms and if branches to reorder emitted code so the common case is first, and cold to move rarely-taken code out of the hot region entirely. Both benefits land on both I-cache locality and branch predictor accuracy. FX spec lists hints like @[optimize(aggressive)] (§21.3) and @[cached] decorators (§17.3) but not branch-level hints. Missing context: how these interact with PGO (gap #145), whether they affect verification (they should not — pure emission hints), and what error is raised when profile data contradicts the programmer's hint.
gaps.prior_art: C++20 [[likely]] / [[unlikely]] as formal attributes on branch statements. GCC __builtin_expect(x, 1) / __builtin_expect_with_probability. Rust core::intrinsics::likely / unlikely (unstable). Linux kernel likely() / unlikely() macros ubiquitous since 2.6. LLVM metadata !prof.branch_weights. Rust RFC 1131 proposes likely / unlikely attributes. Swift @inline(never) for cold paths.

gaps.name: devirtualize_hard_fail_attribute
gaps.description: Performance-critical code sometimes requires a guarantee that a dispatch site compiles to a direct call (zero BTB pressure, fully inlinable). The opposite also matters: CT crypto code may require indirect-call-only emission (no speculative-devirt guard that branches on fn pointer identity, gap #142). Spec has neither. Without these, the programmer cannot express intent to the compiler — today's aggressive devirtualization might regress to tomorrow's indirect call when a new concrete type gets added to the closed world, silently changing hot-path performance. Hard-fail attributes turn such regressions into visible compile errors at the call site.
gaps.prior_art: No mainstream language has a hard-fail devirtualization attribute. Closest: Rust #[inline(always)] hard-requires inlining (but inlining is related, not equivalent); fails compilation if inlining is impossible. Clang pragma 'clang loop vectorize(enable)' fails if vectorization impossible. C++ [[gnu::always_inline]]. Hotspot -XX:+PrintCompilation reports devirt decisions but has no hard-fail. CompCert rejects non-verifiable constructs outright, including some inlining scenarios.

gaps.name: profile_guided_dispatch_optimization
gaps.description: Dispatch emit decisions benefit enormously from profile data — at each call site, which concrete fn pointer dominated the call target distribution? Without PGO, the compiler falls back on static heuristics that miss (e.g., emits indirect call for what was actually 99% monomorphic in production). FX currently has '--release=aggressive' but no PGO. Sketch mode (§22.3) records execution traces to .fxr files — the traces already contain call-target data that PGO needs. The integration path is unspecified: how are traces collected from production runs? How does the compiler consume them? Where does the profile live relative to fx.lock and per-profile settings? Does PGO override programmer hints (gap #143), or the reverse? How does it compose with contract-driven devirtualization (gap #140)?
gaps.prior_art: LLVM PGO (instrumented '-fprofile-instr-generate' + '-fprofile-instr-use'); AutoFDO (sampling-based, merged 2014); CSSPGO (Context-Sensitive Sample PGO, Google 2022); Facebook BOLT post-link optimizer; Propeller (Google post-link); Rust '-C profile-generate' / '-C profile-use'; Swift PGO; .NET tiered compilation with on-stack replacement; HotSpot type profiling via method inline caches.

gaps.name: spectre_v2_dispatch_mitigation
gaps.description: Indirect calls are Spectre v2 targets (Branch Target Injection). Hardware mitigations — Intel CET IBT (Tiger Lake 2020+), ARM BTI (Armv8.5 2020+) — verify target markers at landing sites. Software mitigations — retpoline, IBPB, IBRS, BHB flush on ARM — each have different cost and apply to different indirect-branch categories. Retpoline alone costs ~20 cycles per indirect call. FX spec says nothing about which mitigation is applied by default, whether it's profile-selectable, and how it interacts with CT code (CT timing constraints may conflict with mitigation costs — a retpoline adds deterministic cost but an IBRS barrier flushes the predictor, both observable in timing). Matters for FX's systems-programming target (drivers §19, kernels via C ABI, crypto).
gaps.prior_art: Linux kernel retpoline default (~20 cycles per indirect call) since 2018, CONFIG_MITIGATION_RETPOLINE. CET IBT stabilized Tiger Lake (2020); ARM BTI in Armv8.5 (2020). Rust '-Z retpoline'. Chromium V8 indirect-branch hardening. OpenSSL selects mitigation per-target CPU. Microsoft's 'Speculative Load Hardening' in MSVC. Apple Silicon ARM BTI enforced on kernel. gcc '-mindirect-branch=thunk' / '-mfunction-return=thunk'.

gaps.name: inline_cache_policy_statement
gaps.description: Traditional dynamic languages use inline caches (IC) — record the last concrete target at a dispatch site, patch subsequent calls to direct-call the cached target, deoptimize on mismatch. Fast for polymorphic-but-stable sites. FX's §25.5 (Supply Chain Defenses) explicitly forbids raw pointer access and runtime function-pointer patching, which implicitly rules ICs out — but the spec doesn't state the dispatch implication explicitly. Without a clear policy statement, readers may assume ICs are a future optimization path. The tradeoff is real: megamorphic dispatch sites (≥5 distinct targets over program lifetime) pay indirect-call cost every call without IC amortization. FX accepts this cost in exchange for (a) no runtime code modification attack surface, (b) no deoptimization pauses, (c) verifiable compilation (§20.8) — emitted bytes never change post-link.
gaps.prior_art: Smalltalk-80 inline caches (Deutsch-Schiffman POPL 1984) — the origin. V8's hidden classes + polymorphic ICs (up to 4 entries before megamorphic fallback). LuaJIT's trace-based specialization. PyPy's meta-tracing. Java HotSpot's inline cache megamorphic dispatch via vtable snoop. Contrasted with static-only approaches: Rust, C++, Zig, Swift (all without runtime IC); Rust's lack of JIT is part of its 'no runtime' promise.

gaps.name: overclaim_structural_impossibility
gaps.description: §1.4 'Structural Impossibility' table lists bug classes with the claim they are 'structurally unrepresentable'. External review (effectfully, 2026-04-20) flagged three rows as overclaim: (1) Integer overflow — 'fixed-width up to 1024 bits; overflow mode declared per fn' is redefining overflow, not preventing it; under 'overflow(wrap)' you still wrap; under 'overflow(trap)' you still trap; only 'exact' with a compiler-proved fit prevents overflow and that is conditional. (2) Stack overflow — 'decreases clause proves bounded depth' is correct about termination but does NOT imply the bounded depth fits in the process stack; a function with decreases recursing 10^12 times still blows the stack; §19.5 'Alloc(Stack, bound: N)' is the actual stack-size guarantee and is opt-in. (3) Resource leak — 'linear: every resource must be consumed' is correct for linearly-typed values but a user-level 'resource' (counter that should be decremented, external handle not modelled in the type system, cache entry that should be invalidated) is not protected unless expressed as a linear value; 'leak' is subjective in ways the table does not acknowledge. The remaining eleven rows (buffer overflow, use-after-free, double free, null deref, uninitialized memory, data race, secret in logs, SQL injection, deadlock on session types, FP non-determinism, UB) appear rigorous given their stated mechanisms. Effect on readers: the table reads as marketing; fixing it makes the actual guarantees legible and the residual gaps honest.
gaps.prior_art: Rust's documentation distinguishes 'the type system prevents X' (aliasing xor mutability) from 'Safe Rust prevents X' (double-free via Drop, uninitialized via MaybeUninit discipline) from 'Rust documents X is undefined' (integer overflow is wrapping in release, panic in debug, not UB). Ada/SPARK 2012 separates 'proved absent' (via SPARK static analysis) from 'checked at runtime' (Ada range checks) from 'declared out of scope' (aliasing with Global annotations). Coq's TCB discussion explicitly separates 'kernel-verified' from 'extracted-code-trusted' from 'axiom-tainted'. Lean 4 documents axiom dependencies per definition via #print axioms. The pragmatic pattern across rigorous systems is: say precisely what you prevent, what you surface, what you verify, and what remains user responsibility.
gaps.resolution: Resolved by Chapter 1 rewrite (§1.4 now 'What the Type System Checks'). Three-column table per row: Bug class, Mechanism, Scope of guarantee. Each row now names its scope honestly: 'elaboration' (the unsafe program cannot typecheck), 'compile-time SMT proof' (requires programmer to carry refinements), or 'declared in signature' (the bug class is made visible, not eliminated). Integer overflow split into two rows: unbounded int/nat (never overflows, runtime bignum) vs fixed-width with declared overflow mode (exact requires proof; wrap/trap/saturate surface the behavior rather than remove it). Stack growth split into termination (decreases proves finite depth but not stack fit) and stack size (Alloc(Stack, bound: N) is the actual opt-in guarantee per §19.5). Typed resource leak row explicitly notes user-level 'resources' not expressed as linear values are not tracked. Deadlock row notes mechanism covers session-typed channels only, not arbitrary mutex waits.

gaps.name: unsafe_escape_hatch_rationale
gaps.description: §1.4 states 'There is no unsafe keyword. There is no type-system bypass.' The spec presents this as a bullet in the structural-impossibility list without articulating the design logic. External review (effectfully, 2026-04-20) objected — unsafety is genuinely useful for performance tuning, experimentation, FFI bridges, and unverified-but-correct code. The position is defensible — FX's actual design has a two-tier model: sketch mode (§22) is TypeScript-permissive with runtime checks and all proof obligations deferred; release/compiled mode rejects 'sorry', 'axiom', unproven refinements, and weaker effects. This is a coherent choice driven by supply-chain hygiene (§25.5): agent-written packages should not ship unsafe code that downstream consumers cannot audit. Per-definition escape hatches already exist: 'sorry' for unfinished proofs (rejected in release), 'axiom' for mathematical assumptions (tracked in trust dimension), 'with Div' for non-terminating functions (graduates to Sorry trust). These are FX's typed unsafe — escape hatches with explicit accounting rather than untyped bypass. The spec does not explain this model as an explicit design choice, so readers see only the prohibition and miss the escape hatches that exist.
gaps.prior_art: Rust has 'unsafe' as a blanket keyword with no finer-grained accounting — you opt into all five unsafe superpowers (deref raw pointer, call unsafe fn, access union, access mutable static, impl unsafe trait) together. Haskell's unsafePerformIO is the archetypal typed-but-risky escape, isolated to one function. Agda has '--safe' flag toggling primitive-blessing and primitive-unsafety; terms using '--unsafe' features are marked in the type system. Coq's 'Axiom' and 'Admitted' are tracked per-definition via 'Print Assumptions'. F* has '--admit_smt_queries' for skipping specific proofs with flag-controlled auditability. Lean 4 #print axioms shows per-definition axiom dependencies. Idris 2 multiplicity-0 erased code is unchecked at runtime but typed at compile time. The common thread: typed escapes with per-definition tracking beat untyped blanket escapes.
gaps.resolution: Resolved by new §1.6 'The Two-Tier Safety Model'. The section makes explicit what was previously implicit: FX has no surface-level unsafe keyword but provides typed per-definition escape hatches. Sketch mode (§22) is TypeScript-permissive; release mode requires trust level Verified on every reachable function except on paths marked Assumed. The three escape hatches — sorry, axiom, and 'with Div' on rec — are listed with their semantics, profile availability, and trust propagation. They are FX's answer to unsafe: typed, scoped to a single definition, tracked in the trust dimension, surfaced in diagnostics via 'fxc --show-axioms', and audited at package publish time per §14.10 contract diff and §25.5 supply-chain defenses. Rationale is stated: agent-written packages cannot ship unverified assumptions silently into the supply chain.

gaps.name: intro_marketing_tone
gaps.description: External review (effectfully, 2026-04-20) characterized §1 as overly aspirational / marketing-heavy. Specific issues: §1.2 uses 'mathematically guaranteed', 'correct by construction', 'structurally impossible' without caveats about scope. §1.4 overclaims three rows (see gap #148). 'Every behavior is defined' (§1.2) ignores that behavior depends on declared annotations — an unannotated program has minimum behavior because it cannot do much, not because it is defined in a rich way. The agent-first framing ('primary user is an agentic LLM') reads aspirational until agents actually ship FX in practice. Fixing this does not require abandoning the design principles — it requires precision. 'Correct by construction' is true for bug classes listed if the scope is 'typed linear values declared with appropriate refinements', not 'all programs'. Rigor-first writing would distinguish claims by category: (a) prevented by the type system at parse/elaborate time, (b) surfaced in the signature so the reader/reviewer sees the capability, (c) verified via SMT against declared refinements, (d) declared and checked only at release profile.
gaps.prior_art: Rust's reference and Rustonomicon separate 'what safe Rust guarantees' from 'what unsafe Rust provides escape for' explicitly. The Ada Reference Manual distinguishes legality rules (compile-time), dynamic semantics (runtime), and implementation-defined behavior. TeX's tradition of separating normative specification from user-facing documentation. Lean 4 documentation explicitly labels 'axioms', 'tactics', 'metaprograms' by trust tier. The pattern is: precise technical specification first, marketing prose for external communication kept separate from spec text.
gaps.resolution: Resolved by Chapter 1 rewrite. §1.2 Design Principles converted from ten bold-header marketing bullets to six flowing prose paragraphs. Superlatives dropped: 'mathematically guaranteed', 'correct by construction', 'structurally impossible' are replaced by precise statements with scope ('checked at compile time, not annotated and ignored', 'a theorem of the type system, not a best effort', 'enumerable per-definition rather than global'). 'Every behavior is defined' expanded to list specific commitments (left-to-right eval, overflow mode per signature, strict IEEE 754 default, stable sort default, map iteration order) with cross-references. §1.3 tightened: the encrypt_and_send example now lists each checked property with its dimension number and the consequence of relaxing it. §1.4 (see gap #148) overhauled with three-column mechanism/scope table. §1.5 erasure table kept but prose tightened. New §1.6 articulates the two-tier safety model (sketch permissive / release strict) plus the three typed escape hatches, addressing gap #149's critique. Overall tone shift: from aspiration to specification.

gaps.name: distribution_mode_taxonomy
gaps.description: §25 specifies one distribution model (public registry with transparency log, Minimal Version Selection, semver from contract diff) but real-world FX deployment needs a taxonomy of deployment modes with clear composition rules. Three modes are needed: (a) Solo — single project with path/git/public deps, no infrastructure required, works offline from first fetch, targets hobbyist and single-project scope; (b) Workspace — multi-package unified tree under one fx.workspace.toml with shared content-addressed cache, shared trust policy, shared signing authority, FX-native build driver ('fxc workspace'), scales from 5-dev team to 10k-dev monorepo with the same mechanism; (c) Public — the existing §25 registry-backed ecosystem with Sigstore-style transparency log. Composition rules matter: a Workspace typically consumes Public deps for stdlib; a Solo project graduates to Workspace by running 'fxc init --workspace'; a Workspace graduates packages to Public by running 'fxc publish' with stricter policy checks. The gap is that §25 today conflates these — there is no workspace concept, no directory-as-module identity, and no explicit composition story. Big corps and startups both need Workspace; the spec currently has neither. Integrity constraint: every mode must use the identical kernel (§31), the identical 21-dim type system, the identical trust semantics (§10.6), and must never require bypassing the type system. Modes differ in deployment infrastructure, never in language semantics. An agent-written package compiled under any mode produces semantically identical output; the agent does not need to know which mode is active.
gaps.prior_art: Cargo/crates.io (Rust) has an implicit Solo/Workspace/Public split: cargo workspace aggregates multiple crates under one Cargo.toml with a shared target/ directory, but treats trust and distribution uniformly across all three. Maven Central + corporate Nexus + ~/.m2 (Java) is the closest trio with organizational separation, though trust semantics are ad-hoc. Nix flakes (nixos.org RFC 49) content-address every artifact and allow any flake to function in any mode — the most principled prior art on mode-independent artifacts; Guix extends with capability-based channels. Go modules treat GitHub paths as module identity, eliminating the separate registry entirely (Mode S via URL imports); Deno takes this further with URL-only imports and no registry at all. Hackage + stack snapshots + local snapshots (Haskell) provide the cleanest Mode P + Workspace model, though trust is implicit. Opam + opam-repository (OCaml) is minimal and clean for Solo + Public, with no workspace concept. Turborepo + Nx (JavaScript) ship Workspace-only with remote caching. Bazel / Buck2 / Pants are Workspace-only at enterprise scale with strong hermeticity but poor Solo / Public stories. The pattern across all prior art: no language offers clean three-mode taxonomy with distribution-independent trust semantics. FX's opportunity: the 21-dimension type system (uniform across modes) plus per-definition trust (uniform across modes) plus content-addressed artifacts makes taxonomy-by-infrastructure-only possible. No existing language has demonstrated this because none has FX's kernel-axiomatic foundation.
gaps.resolution: Resolved by §25.1 'Deployment Modes' introducing Mode S (Solo), Mode W (Workspace), Mode P (Public). Every mode uses identical kernel semantics, identical type system, identical trust model — the difference is infrastructure (none / FX-native workspace driver / publication pipeline with transparency log). Composition is explicit: a project declares per-dependency which mode each comes from. Transitions documented: 'fxc init --workspace' graduates Solo to Workspace; 'fxc publish' graduates a workspace package to Public. §25.2-§25.4 specify each mode's tooling in detail. The agent-first integrity constraint holds: an agent emits FX source without knowing which mode will consume it.

gaps.name: fx_workspace_model
gaps.description: The Workspace mode (gap #151) is the defining deployment artifact for teams and enterprise orgs. It needs a complete specification covering: (a) fx.workspace.toml schema declaring workspace root, module map, cache locations, remote cache endpoints, policy files, signing configuration, external non-FX dependencies; (b) directory-as-module identity using '//' paths ('//security/crypto' maps to workspace_root/security/crypto/); (c) module boundaries via package.fx.toml at each directory with public/private API surface; (d) content-addressed build cache with BLAKE3 hashing over (source bytes + build profile + dep-closure hash), local cache at .fx/cache/, remote cache via HTTP GET/PUT protocol (no custom server required — static file serving, S3-compatible blob store, or HTTP object store all work); (e) hermetic build semantics — nothing outside the workspace root is read at build time, external deps are vendored or declared as fx_external; (f) parallel build driver walking the dependency DAG with configurable job count; (g) verification-aware incrementality exploiting §10.13 checkpoints — editing one function re-verifies only affected proof obligations, not transitive closure; (h) offline mode ('fxc workspace build --offline' succeeds if cache populated); (i) 'fxc workspace prefetch' populates cache without building, enabling air-gapped deployment via cache transfer. No integration with Bazel, Buck2, Pants, Make, CMake, or any other existing build system — FX's workspace driver is FX-native, stands alone, and competes with those systems on their own turf (speed, hermeticity, cache protocols) rather than layering on top of them. Integrity constraint: hermeticity is the supply-chain boundary (§25.5 extends naturally to Workspace). Content-addressed artifacts preserve §20.8 end-to-end correctness theorem. Verification-aware incrementality preserves proof stability (§29.1). The workspace trust story subsumes the project-profile story of §25.6 without replacing it — the two compose.
gaps.prior_art: Bazel (Google, 2015) is the reigning gold standard for monorepo-scale hermetic builds. Its Remote Build Execution (RBE) protocol and content-addressed Action Cache are widely emulated. Criticisms: BUILD files are a Starlark DSL programmers must learn separately; polyglot by design so every language ships a rules_* repo; configuration churn across Bazel versions. Buck2 (Meta, 2023 open-sourced) is Bazel's modern successor: better remote cache semantics, Starlark-based, aggressive about hermeticity. Pants (Toolchain Labs, v2 2021+) is the Python-originated peer with strong Python ergonomics and language-agnostic v2 architecture. Cargo workspace (Rust) is the minimalist counterpoint: simple, single-language, workspace-only, no remote cache primitive. Turborepo (Vercel, 2021+) and Nx (Nrwl, 2018+) handle JavaScript monorepos with remote cache as a paid feature. Nix flakes content-address every derivation with the strongest reproducibility theorem of any prior art; workspace-equivalent is a multi-flake repository. Guix (GNU) is the Scheme-based alternative to Nix with similar reproducibility guarantees. Dune (OCaml) is project-local only, no remote cache. sbt (Scala) and Gradle (Kotlin/Java) have workspace concepts but prioritize flexibility over hermeticity. Tup and Ninja are low-level incremental build engines usable under higher-level systems. The FX workspace differentiator: first system to make workspace hermeticity simultaneously (1) the supply-chain trust boundary, (2) the proof-stability boundary (§29.1), (3) content-addressed at artifact level AND at attestation level. Bazel has (1) partially via remote execution sandboxing but no (2) or (3); Nix has (3) but not (1) in the trust-dimension sense; no existing system has (2) because no existing system has FX's graded verification framework.
gaps.resolution: Resolved by §25.3 'Mode W: Workspaces'. Specifies fx.workspace.toml schema, '//' directory-as-module path syntax, package.fx.toml at each directory, content-addressed cache, hermetic build semantics, verification-aware incremental rebuild exploiting §10.13 checkpoints, offline / prefetch operations for air-gapped deployment. Build driver 'fxc workspace build' is FX-native with no Bazel/Buck/Pants/Make/CMake integration. Integrity constraints preserved: hermeticity is the supply-chain boundary; content-addressing preserves the §20.8 correctness theorem; verification-aware incrementality preserves proof stability per §29.1.

gaps.name: polyglot_interop_via_artifacts
gaps.description: Orgs that keep their existing C/C++/Rust/Go/Java/Python build systems need a frictionless way to consume FX output. Spec needs to commit to artifact-boundary interop — NOT to writing FX-side plugins for Bazel, Buck, CMake, Make, cargo, Go, Gradle, SBT, or any other external build system. FX produces standard artifacts any build system can swallow: 'fxc build --emit=static_lib --abi=c' yields .a + header + manifest; '--emit=shared_lib' yields .so/.dll/.dylib + header + manifest; '--emit=object' yields raw .o; '--emit=wasm' yields .wasm + .wit (WASM Component Model interface); '--emit=binary' yields standalone executable. The JSON manifest file is the critical addition: it records the FX trust aggregate (direct + transitive), the axiom list with signers, the sorry count with locations, the declared effect set, the refinements on exported types, and the symbol→C-ABI-signature map. Legacy consumers ignore the manifest; trust-aware security pipelines read and act on it. Consumer-side rule is unchanged from existing conventions: 'cc_library(srcs = [':libfoo.a'], hdrs = [':foo.h'])' in Bazel, 'target_link_libraries(app foo)' in CMake, '#cgo LDFLAGS: -lfoo' in Go, 'build.rs' + '#[link]' in Rust, 'ctypes.CDLL' in Python. Each case is what those ecosystems already do with any prebuilt C library — FX is just another such library producer. FX never learns any of these systems' DSLs. The @[abi(C)] attribute on pub fn (§19.10) already covers the source-level export direction; the gap is in the emit-format catalog, the manifest schema, the symbol-mangling rules across languages, and the ABI-versioning story (ensuring consumers don't need to rebuild on every FX compiler update). Integrity constraint: the manifest preserves the trust-dimension information that would otherwise be lost crossing the artifact boundary. Even if a legacy consumer ignores the manifest, the information is present and auditable by anything that does care. This is FX's export of its trust model to the rest of the software world.
gaps.prior_art: Rust's cbindgen + cargo (crate-type = [staticlib, cdylib]) is the most relevant and widely-used prior art: 'cargo build' produces .a or .so, cbindgen generates C headers from Rust source annotations, millions of deployed binaries use this flow (Firefox, Chrome parts, Linux kernel drivers). Go cgo with '-buildmode=c-archive' or '-buildmode=c-shared' produces .a/.so + header; widely used for Docker, Kubernetes tooling. Zig is designed for C interop from day one, matches FX's philosophy closely. Swift's @_cdecl exports C ABI and generates Obj-C + C headers. Kotlin/Native cinterop handles both import and export. Nim's {.exportc.} pragma marks symbols for C export. Haskell FFI is mature but has a heavy runtime dependency. OCaml's ctypes + OCaml-as-library is fiddly but works. .NET Native AOT (shipped .NET 8, 2023) compiles C# to native with C ABI exports — Microsoft's bet on native interop. GraalVM Native Image does the same for Java. For WASM: the Component Model with WIT (WebAssembly Interface Types) is the emerging standard for high-level polyglot interop — every exported function has a typed ABI declared in WIT, and consumers in any language generate bindings. wit-bindgen is the generator. Manifest prior art: pkg-config .pc files declare library metadata; CMake package config files do similar; cargo metadata outputs structured JSON about a Rust build; SPDX and CycloneDX are the SBOM (Software Bill of Materials) standards that security pipelines consume. FX's manifest should extend SBOM conventions by adding the trust-aggregate, axiom-list, and sorry-count fields — no other language's artifacts carry this information because no other language has the trust dimension to export. This positions FX as the first language whose artifacts are auditable for verification status via standard SBOM-consuming tooling.
gaps.resolution: Resolved by §25.7 'Polyglot Interop via Artifacts'. Specifies five emit formats (static_lib, shared_lib, object, wasm, binary) with accompanying .h headers and .manifest.json files. The JSON manifest carries the trust aggregate, axiom list with signers, sorry count, declared effects, and symbol→C-ABI signature map — extending SBOM conventions (SPDX, CycloneDX) so legacy security pipelines can read FX trust information. ABI versioning via 'fxc abi-compat'. Consumer-side integration uses existing build-system conventions (cc_library, target_link_libraries, cgo, build.rs) with no FX-side plugin required. @[abi(C)] and @[export] attributes control the source-level export direction (§19.10). FX never ships plugins for Bazel / Buck / CMake / cargo / etc.; the boundary is artifact-level.

gaps.name: staged_migration_path
gaps.description: Orgs adopting FX in a legacy codebase need a documented staged migration path with invariants at each stage, so each stage is a stable operating point (not a transitional state under time pressure). Five stages: (1) Island — one FX module inside a legacy codebase, exported as static lib, consumed by legacy build; no workspace needed. (2) Archipelago — several FX modules across the codebase, unified under one fx.workspace.toml, shared cache and policy, still exported as individual libs; legacy build remains top-level orchestrator. (3) Symbiosis — major subsystems are FX, workspace is a peer to the legacy build system; policy tightens (security team owns security/*, crypto requires attestation); noticing FX's cache and verification-aware incrementality as quality-of-life improvements. (4) Inversion — new services are FX-native; legacy modules still compile via legacy but are declared as extern fx_external deps in the FX workspace, letting fxc orchestrate end-to-end; CI runs 'fxc workspace build' which subcontracts to legacy builders for non-FX code. (5) Pure — no legacy build system remaining; FX workspace IS the monorepo; security team audits via 'fxc audit //...'. Critical property: every stage is a valid final state. An org that lives at Stage 2 for five years is fine. There is no FX-side deprecation pressure to advance. This is the pragmatic answer to 'big corps that don't care' — they can live at Stage 1 forever if their legacy is load-bearing, and still benefit from type safety on the one critical module. This is not a spec section (which specifies language semantics) but a canonical deployment guide that ships with FX, referenced from §25. Integrity constraint: each stage preserves per-definition trust tracking (§10.6); sketch/release profiles (§22) work identically at every stage; supply-chain hygiene (§25.5) applies consistently; agent-authored code flows through any stage without modification; no stage requires bypassing the type system. The migration never asks the org to temporarily suspend verification or trust rules.
gaps.prior_art: Rust in the Linux kernel (Rust-for-Linux project, officially merged 2022) is the canonical living example of Stage 1 / Island migration: individual Rust drivers (Binder, NVMe, Apple M1 GPU) coexist with C kernel; Rust exports no pub API to C code, imports kernel C APIs via bindgen; multi-year effort with no finish date announced. Kotlin in Android (Google, 2017+) shows Stage 3-4 trajectory: Java still works, Kotlin is now primary for new code; Google's gradual tightening from 'Kotlin supported' (2017) to 'Kotlin preferred' (2019) to 'Kotlin-first' (2020+) is the playbook for a successful migration with long coexistence. TypeScript in JavaScript projects (Microsoft, 2012+) started as sidecar .d.ts annotation files and grew to full TS adoption; the gradual-typing pattern is widely documented. Go's adoption inside Google involved decade-long migration from C++ for service-layer code, with clean interop at RPC boundaries rather than ABI level. Swift in iOS (Apple, 2014+) is Apple's curated adoption: Objective-C coexists and still works; Swift is preferred for new code; Apple controls the compilers. ClojureScript demonstrated gradual adoption within JavaScript codebases via first-class JS interop. Scala at Twitter migrated off Java over years; the eventual transition away from Scala to Rust for some services shows migrations can reverse — a humbling data point. Rust-for-Linux itself is a warning: even a beloved new language fails to dominate when embedded in a 30-million-line C kernel; coexistence is permanent, not transitional. Common pattern across successful adoptions: (1) legacy survives indefinitely, (2) no mandatory rewrites, (3) clean interop at an existing standard boundary (C ABI, RPC, wire protocol), (4) incentives for new code favor the new language without forbidding the old, (5) escape hatch to continue using legacy is always available. Failed adoptions invariably violated one of these rules (D vs C++ in the 2000s, Haskell vs Perl in the 2010s, purely academic languages at large companies). FX's commitment to artifact-boundary interop (gap #153) and workspace model (gap #152) together enable the successful-adoption pattern; this gap documents the playbook.
gaps.resolution: Resolved by §25.8 'Staged Migration from Legacy'. Documents five stages (Island, Archipelago, Symbiosis, Inversion, Pure) with each stage described as a stable final state. Cross-references Rust-for-Linux as the Stage 1 prior art trajectory. Sketch/release mode coexistence at directory level is made explicit. The integrity constraint holds at every stage: per-definition trust tracking, sketch/release profiles, supply-chain hygiene, agent-authored code flow all unchanged across stages. No stage requires bypassing the type system.

gaps.name: workspace_policy_and_signing
gaps.description: Workspace mode (gap #152) needs a policy mechanism that controls what can happen inside each directory subtree, plus a signing mechanism that establishes who vouches for each piece of code. Policy files (proposed name: policy.fxpolicy) live at directory granularity, parse as FX expressions (no new syntax to learn), and compose via inheritance down the tree — a subdirectory can tighten but not loosen its ancestor's policy. Example constraints a policy expresses: trust_floor = Verified (every function in this subtree must reach trust level Verified); allowed_effects = [Tot, CT, Alloc(Stack)] (no IO, no Alloc(Heap) permitted); max_sorry = 0 (no sorry markers anywhere in this subtree); max_axioms = 3 (at most three declared axioms); required_signers = [@security-team, @crypto-audit] (attestations must be signed by both roles); consumable_from = ['//products/*'] (only paths matching this pattern may import from this directory). Signing uses three distinct roles: author (individual developer, signs their commit), owner (directory owner per OWNERS-style file, signs on behalf of team accountability), auditor (third-party review, signs independently). Consumer policy specifies which role combinations are acceptable for which trust tier. Corporate PKI integration: signing identities backed by hardware keys (HSM via PKCS#11), OIDC-issued short-lived tokens (GitHub OIDC, GCP workload identity, AWS IAM Roles Anywhere), SSH keys (gitconfig commit signing), WebAuthn/FIDO2 for interactive signing at developer laptops, PIV smart cards for government/defense contractor identity. FX does not invent new key infrastructure — it speaks standard signing protocols (Sigstore for transparency log, in-toto for attestations, ITE-4 for metadata, SLSA for build integrity levels). Integrity constraint: policy is code — parsed by fxc, checked at build time, version-controlled alongside source. No trust backdoor: policy CAN relax trust (allow sorry, allow axiom) but only per explicit declaration that an auditor signs off on. Every signer identity is recorded in the content-addressed transparency log and enumerable via 'fxc audit'. The trust dimension (§10.6) is the kernel-level source of truth; policy is the organizational-level enforcement layer; signatures are the accountability layer — three layers with clear separation of concerns.
gaps.prior_art: CODEOWNERS (GitHub, 2017) maps repository paths to teams for PR review; used by thousands of orgs but provides no build-time enforcement and no trust concept. Bazel BUILD visibility (public/private per-package) constrains imports but is not about trust or verification. OPA / Open Policy Agent (CNCF, 2018+) with the Rego language provides general-purpose policy-as-code; Kubernetes uses it for admission control; widely deployed but overkill for FX's narrower need. Sigstore (OpenSSF, 2021+) is the canonical stack for the signing half: Fulcio (OIDC identity → short-lived X.509 certs), Rekor (append-only transparency log), Cosign (sign/verify tooling). Widely adopted — npm, PyPI, Kubernetes releases, GitHub Actions all use Sigstore components. in-toto (CNCF, 2018+) specifies supply-chain attestation metadata format; ITE-4 is the current relevant specification. SLSA (Supply Chain Levels for Software Artifacts, Google-led 2021+) defines L1–L4 of build integrity with increasing requirements; FX trust tiers align naturally (roughly: Sorry→L1, Tested→L2, Verified→L3–L4). TUF / The Update Framework (2010) pioneered key delegation for package updates; PyPI uses it for package signing. WebAuthn / FIDO2 is the hardware-key standard; YubiKey, Google Titan, Apple Touch ID all implement. PKCS#11 is the HSM standard interface; AWS CloudHSM, Azure Key Vault, GCP Cloud HSM all speak it. Notary v2 (OCI project) provides signing for container images; architectural cousin of what FX needs for FX artifacts. FX's differentiator: policy files are not a new DSL (like Rego or Starlark) but reuse FX's own grammar; every policy constraint can reference FX types, effects, dimensions, and trust levels directly; the workspace cache and build driver check policy during build so violations fail fast. Every signer role and signature is auditable via the same 'fxc --show-axioms'-style per-definition query. No existing system combines policy-as-FX-expression with per-definition trust propagation with multi-role signatures in the transparency log; FX would be the first.
gaps.resolution: Resolved by §25.9 'Workspace Policy and Signing'. policy.fxpolicy files parse as FX expressions (no separate DSL), inherit down the directory tree with tighten-only semantics. Three signer roles specified: Author (individual), Owner (team via OWNERS file), Auditor (third-party). Consumer policy picks role combinations per trust tier. Corporate PKI integration enumerates Sigstore (Fulcio/Rekor/Cosign), PKCS#11 HSMs, SSH signing keys, WebAuthn/FIDO2 hardware keys, PIV smart cards — standard protocols FX speaks, not custom inventions. Attestations formatted per in-toto ITE-4 and logged in Sigstore Rekor or an internal transparency log. Policy is code — parsed, reviewed, and checked at every build.

gaps.name: reproducible_and_distributed_builds
gaps.description: FX's §20.8 end-to-end correctness theorem claims 'well-typed program + fixed input = defined observable output'. To extend this from single-machine compilation to distributed build at corporate scale requires content-addressing every artifact, a deterministic build output theorem, a remote cache protocol, vendoring, and offline operation. Specific mechanisms: (a) every compilation artifact (.fxc, .fxl, .fxa attestation, .o, .a, .so) is content-addressed by BLAKE3-256 hash over (source bytes, compiler version, profile, full dep closure hash) — identical inputs guarantee identical outputs. (b) remote cache protocol over standard HTTP — GET /artifact/{hash} returns the artifact or 404; PUT /artifact/{hash} uploads (authentication per org policy); no special cache server required; static file servers, S3-compatible blob stores, Artifactory, plain nginx all serve. (c) 'fxc workspace vendor' flattens all remote deps into workspace_root/vendor/, switching resolution from network to filesystem; mandatory for Mode 3 enterprise hermetic builds, optional for Mode 2, useful for Mode 1 reproducibility. (d) 'fxc workspace prefetch' populates the local cache from remote without building — enables 'build once in connected environment, transfer cache, build in air-gapped environment' flow critical for defense, financial, healthcare deployments. (e) 'fxc workspace build --offline' fails loudly if any required artifact is missing from cache rather than silently fetching. (f) determinism theorem — for any FX source + compiler version + profile + dep closure, compilation produces byte-identical .fxc/.fxl/.fxa/.o output on any host with any timezone / locale / filesystem / hardware that supports the target architecture. BLAKE3 chosen over SHA-256 for throughput (~6 GB/s on modern CPUs vs SHA-256's ~500 MB/s) — on large workspaces the cache hash bandwidth matters. Integrity constraint: content-addressing is the supply-chain trust boundary at artifact level; cache entries cannot be forged without breaking the hash; the transparency log (§25.3) records every published artifact by hash; consumers verify hashes against the log before trusting any cache entry. Trust tiers (§10.6) are embedded in the artifact metadata — downstream can filter cache by 'only Verified artifacts' for release builds. Air-gapped deployment is genuinely air-gapped: the prefetch output is a self-contained directory transferable over any medium (USB drive, write-once media, optical disc), reproducibility verifies bit-identical output.
gaps.prior_art: Nix (NixOS, 2003+) is the reference implementation of content-addressed derivations. Every build step is a derivation keyed by its inputs' hashes; identical inputs guarantee identical outputs (modulo non-determinism in tools). Widely deployed (NixOS, Nix-on-macOS, Nix for CI). The rigorous reproducibility theorem of FX's §20.8 requires a Nix-grade substrate. Guix (GNU, 2012+) is the Scheme-based alternative with identical philosophy, slightly different interface. Bazel Remote Cache + Remote Build Execution (Google, 2015+) specifies an HTTP/gRPC protocol (Remote Execution API) for content-addressed caching at enterprise scale; widely deployed in Google, Stripe, Uber, etc. The RE-API is the natural prior art for FX's cache protocol but is more complex than FX needs (supports arbitrary action execution, not just artifact fetch). Turborepo (Vercel, 2021+) ships simple HTTP remote cache for JS monorepos — the minimum viable version and closest to what FX should adopt. BuildBuddy and EngFlow are commercial RE-API-compatible build services. Docker's layer cache is pragmatic but not reproducible by default (timestamps, random IDs leak into layers). Reproducible Builds project (reproducible-builds.org, 2013+) is the cross-language effort to make builds bit-identical; tests Debian, Fedora, Arch, and many individual projects. SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH is the widely-adopted environment variable standard for deterministic timestamps. BLAKE3 (O'Connor et al., 2020) is the modern hash function replacing SHA-256 in performance-sensitive contexts; widely adopted (rsync, B3Sum, Zig compiler, Rust's crater). Nixpkgs Hydra is a large-scale reproducible build farm serving multi-million derivations. For offline / air-gapped: cargo vendor (Rust) is the best-known command for flattening deps to a directory; the go module proxy protocol (proxy.golang.org) is a simple HTTP protocol anyone can implement; Python pip wheelhouse is the closest analog. FX's opportunity: first language where reproducibility, content-addressing, AND per-definition trust propagation are orthogonal properties composed rather than features bolted onto a legacy build system. Cache entries carry trust tier and attestation — a cache that's been tampered with breaks the hash; a cache serving unsigned artifacts gets rejected by release-mode consumers. This is FX's answer to the Linux-distribution reproducibility story and to the supply-chain attestation story simultaneously — both are free consequences of the 21-dim type system + workspace model + §25.3 transparency log composing correctly.
gaps.resolution: Resolved by §25.10 'Reproducibility and Distributed Builds'. Specifies BLAKE3-256 content-addressing, remote cache HTTP protocol (GET/HEAD/PUT with hash-named artifacts, works over any static file server or S3-compatible blob store), 'fxc workspace vendor' / 'prefetch' / '--offline' operations, determinism theorem (pure compile function with SOURCE_DATE_EPOCH for timestamps and content-hash-derived randomness), cache entries carrying trust tier so release-grade consumers reject cache hits below their required trust level. Integrity constraints: content-addressing is the supply-chain boundary; cache poisoning requires breaking both hash and transparency log; §20.8 end-to-end correctness theorem extends naturally to distributed builds.

gaps.name: per_dimension_grade_flow_send_recv
gaps.description: fx_design.md §11 specifies session types operationally — channels advance per Send/Recv events, payloads are passed — but the per-dimension grade-vector update at each event is not stated as a single typing rule. Without the rule, every per-dimension behavior (Tagged provenance through protocols, Refined predicates as payload subsorts, Secret declassification at the wire, fractional-permission CSL frame at session boundaries) requires bespoke case analysis at every consumer. Downstream realizations (Crucible's ~600-LoC PermissionedSessionHandle template in C++) reconstruct by template metaprogramming what FX should state once as a kernel rule.
gaps.prior_art: The session-types literature establishes the rule piecemeal. Caires-Pfenning CONCUR 2010 establishes the linear-logic correspondence: session types are intuitionistic linear propositions, send is ⊗, receive is ⊸, branch is &, select is ⊕. Wadler ICFP 2012 / JFP 2014 popularizes propositions-as-sessions. Honda-Yoshida-Carbone POPL 2008 extends to multiparty with global types and projection. Pischke-Masters-Yoshida 2025 (PMY25) gives the asynchronous extension with bounded queue types σ and balanced+ well-formedness. Hou-Yoshida-Kuhn 2024 (HYK24 Theorem 5.8) proves the association invariant Δ ⊑_s G is preserved by reduction — the load-bearing soundness theorem for top-down session typing. Apiroṣ et al. ITP 2025 (smpst-sr-smer) mechanizes synchronous + precise-subtyping subject reduction in Coq. Iris (Jung-Krebbers-Birkedal et al. JFP 2018) provides the BI algebraic core that the per-dimension Usage composition reduces to via §6.4 fractional permissions. Crucible's 24_04_2026_safety_integration.md §5 documents the manual realization in C++ as the framework's central thesis. None of these prior systems unify the per-dimension flow rule across 24 graded dimensions in a single typing judgment — FX is the first to do so by leveraging the uniform 5-tier modality kernel.
gaps.resolution: Resolved by §11.3 'Per-Dimension Grade Flow at Session Events'. The G-Session-Send rule decomposes the grade-vector update by dimension tier: Tier S commutative semiring uses ⊞ (parallel composition); Tier L lattice uses ⊔ with valid_d check; Tier T (Protocol) uses typestate transition K; Tier F (Type, Refinement) uses substitution; Tier V (Version) uses adapter resolution. The dual rule G-Session-Recv updates the receiver's grade vector by the same per-dimension composition with sign reversal on Usage. Subsumes the three Crucible payload markers (Transferable, Borrowed, Returned) as particular vector configurations. Soundness: preserves the HYK24 association invariant; mechanization commitment in lean/FX/Errata/PerDimSendRecv.lean.

gaps.name: trust_boundary_auto_tagging_provenance
gaps.description: fx_design.md §10.8 specifies trust boundaries with auto-generated runtime validators for refinements; §12.3 specifies taint tracking with sanitize calls. The two mechanisms are not unified: a function declared 'with IO, Network, Fail' may return a value of type T that the rest of the system cannot distinguish from 'internally constructed T' versus 'received from the network and not validated'. Manual realizations (Crucible's Tagged<T, source::FromX> markers across five concrete boundaries: Vessel FFI, Cipher cold-tier, CNTP per-layer, syscalls, configuration parsing) reconstruct ad hoc what a unified rule should provide automatically.
gaps.prior_art: Jif (Cornell, Myers et al.) for security-typed information flow with explicit principal labels and declassification. FlowCaml (Simonet 2003) for OCaml-based label inference. Crucible's own Tagged<T, source::*> pattern as the manual realization across five boundaries. Quan dissertation (Duke, ECOOP 2024) demonstrates relaxed noninterference reducing spurious violations by 90% via the faceted-values approach. The Provenance dimension (dim 8) of FX's existing 24-dim system already provides the lattice; what is missing is the auto-tagging discipline at every effect operation in {IO, FFI, Network, MMIO, DB, Deserialize}.
gaps.resolution: Resolved by §10.8 'Trust Boundary Auto-Tagging'. Rule G-Trust-Boundary: every effect operation in {IO, FFI, Network, MMIO, DB, Deserialize} that produces a value of type T grants its result Provenance grade External (Tier S dim 8). The compiler refuses to consume an External-graded value via any API requiring Sanitized, Validated, or Verified provenance. Three escape mechanisms: (a) declared sanitize(class, value, escaper: fn) per §12.3 producing Sanitized; (b) declared validator (§14.5) producing a contract-validated typed value; (c) explicit declassify(value, policy: P) per §12.4. The Provenance lattice (Source(name), Sanitized, Validated, Verified, External, Unknown) admits subtyping: Sanitized flows where External is accepted but not the reverse.

gaps.name: staleness_dimension_22nd
gaps.description: Async-first ML training (Decoupled DiLoCo, INTELLECT-2, HALoS, DisTrO/Psyche) operates with bounded staleness between gradient computation and aggregation. Convergence behavior depends on the product η · λ_max(H) · τ where τ is the staleness; this product is cross-corroborated by three independent fields (martingale theory, thermodynamics, spin-glass dynamics). FX previously had no dimension to track staleness. Without it, async-first training cannot pass the bit-exact replay invariant, async DiLoCo cannot be expressed at the type level, and the admission rule must be implemented as a runtime check.
gaps.prior_art: Mishchenko, Bach, Even, Woodworth, 'Asynchronous SGD Beats Minibatch SGD Under Arbitrary Delays' (arXiv:2206.07638). Yu, Chen, Poor, 'ASGD with Staleness: SDDE Framework' (arXiv:2406.11159, IEEE TSP 2025). Adibi & Dal Fabbro, 'Batch Async Stochastic Approximation with Bounded Delay' (arXiv:2109.03445, v7 Sep 2025). Bäckström et al., 'ASAP.SGD' (PMLR 2022). ASGD non-convex block-coordinate (arXiv:2505.10322). Cross-field: thermodynamics (arXiv:2503.01200, Wasserstein dissipation bound); spin-glass (Ziyin arXiv:2502.05300). No prior language elevates staleness to a graded type-system dimension.
gaps.resolution: Resolved by §6.3 dim 22 'Staleness'. Tier S commutative semiring (ℕ ∪ {∞}, min, +, ∞, 0). Surface annotation on values: Stale<T, τ> is T at staleness grade τ. Surface annotation on functions: 'with Stale(τ_max)' declares the maximum admissible staleness. Composition: a value of grade τ_1 combined with a value of grade τ_2 yields τ_1 + τ_2 (sequential); the maximum is the parallel-composition result. Admission rule: a function consuming a staleness-graded value can declare a precondition 'pre η * λ_max(H) * τ ≤ critical;' discharged by SMT at the call site. Composes pointwise with the existing dimensions.

gaps.name: energy_latency_budget_stdlib_dims
gaps.description: Per-op precision-budget calibrators (e.g. allocating per-op precision by linear program subject to declared energy and latency budgets) need a canonical declaration pattern. Energy and Latency are wall-clock measurements that depend on hardware, scheduling, and IO patterns the kernel does not model — they cannot be discharged at compile time the way Staleness or Usage can. FX therefore ships them as user-defined Tier-S dimensions in Std.Budget rather than promoting them to kernel built-ins. The compiler propagates declared bounds through composition pointwise but does not verify the underlying physical claim; runtime validation hooks into the §23.5 benchmark harness.
gaps.prior_art: Resource-Bounded Type Theory: Compositional Cost Analysis via Graded Modalities (arXiv:2512.06952, Dec 2025). A Graded Modal Type Theory for Pulse Schedules (arXiv:2510.03130). Coeffect literature: Petricek-Orchard-Mycroft ICFP 2014, Choudhury-Krishnaswami ICFP 2020, Gaboardi-Katsumata-Orchard ICFP 2016 'Combining Effects and Coeffects via Grading'. Annotation-only resource tracking precedent: Rust's `#[cfg(target_pointer_width)]` style metadata, C++ `[[carries_dependency]]`, Ada SPARK pre/post-conditions over time bounds.
gaps.resolution: Resolved by §6.6 stdlib pattern: Std.Budget ships Energy, Latency, Power, WallClock, BitsTransferred as user-defined Tier-S dimensions declared via the standard grade_dimension mechanism. Surface usage 'with Energy(<= E), Latency(<= L)' propagates declared bounds pointwise like any user-defined Tier-S dimension; the compiler does not verify the bounds against any hardware or runtime measurement — wall-clock guarantees are an annotation discipline, not a kernel proof obligation. Builds that need empirical validation hook into §23.5 benchmarks. Energy and Latency are NOT kernel built-ins because the compiler cannot statically prove wall-clock claims.

gaps.name: replay_ordering_refinement_async_sessions
gaps.description: Async-first training requires recording the order of nondeterministic events (gradient arrivals, spot evictions, peer reconnects) for bit-exact reproducibility. Session types previously carried no replay metadata; the bit-exact guarantee was restricted to synchronous training. Manual realizations (Crucible's Cipher::ReplayLog<Ordering>, ~16 bytes/event) reconstruct ad hoc what a Provenance refinement on the channel type should provide automatically.
gaps.prior_art: MPIWIZ subgroup reproducible replay (Tsinghua PACMAN). Actor-language record-replay (arXiv:1805.06267). Mishchenko et al. (arXiv:2206.07638). Decoupled DiLoCo (DeepMind 2026). Crucible 25_04_2026.md §4 (the manual realization). No direct FX precedent — FX is the first to lift replay-tag semantics into the session-type system.
gaps.resolution: Resolved by §11 'Replay-Ordering Refinement on Async Sessions'. Surface syntax: 'session<replay: tag> ... end session;' declares that the channel carries an ordering log named 'tag'. Per-event behavior: each Send/Recv emits an OrderingEvent carrying step, source, vector clock, staleness tag, event kind, content hash. The replay log is content-addressed; replay reads events in their recorded total order. Composes with Async effect (every Async operation crossing the channel records an event) and Staleness dim 22 (events carry the staleness grade at admission time).

gaps.name: reversibility_law_structure_class
gaps.description: fx_design.md §16.6 declares @[structure] class with laws including commutativity, associativity, identity. The recognized laws did not include inverse_left and inverse_right, which are required to express MDM-OC orthogonal-delta merging — a key primitive for GDPR weight retraction in ML training. Without the law, the orthogonal-merge discipline cannot be type-checked at the structure-class level.
gaps.prior_art: Modular Delta Merging with Orthogonal Constraints (arXiv:2507.20997). Wait-Free Replicated Data Types and Fair Reconciliation (arXiv:2508.18193, ECOOP 2025). Optimizing CRDTs for Low-Memory Environments (ECOOP 2025). Abstract algebra (Abelian group inverse axioms).
gaps.resolution: Resolved by §16.6 'Reversibility Laws'. @[structure] class recognized laws extended with 'inverse_left : forall x y. sub(add(x, y), y) == x' and 'inverse_right : forall x y. sub(add(x, y), x) == y'. Stdlib instances: ReversibleCommMonoid<OrthogonalDelta<axis, T>> witnesses orthogonal-delta merging. SMT discharge at instance declaration site (P006 on failure). Composes with linearity: reversible operations on linear values consume both arguments and produce a linear result.

gaps.name: reach_modality_container_kinds
gaps.description: FX previously had no formal way to express Linear<Container<Linear<T>>> — a container of linear elements whose container itself owns its elements with the correct capability tracking. Manual realizations (hand-audit of permission-tagged containers like TraceRing, MetaLog) substitute for what a kind-level reach annotation should provide. System Capless (Xu-Bračevac-Pham-Odersky OOPSLA 2025) introduces reach capabilities (rcaps) that name 'what's in the box' for nested generics; 90% of Scala's stdlib collections work under capture tracking with rcaps; the discipline is portable to FX.
gaps.prior_art: Boruch-Gruszecki et al., 'Capturing Types' (TOPLAS 2023). Xu, Bračevac, Pham, Odersky, 'What's in the Box: Ergonomic and Expressive Capture Tracking over Generic Data Structures' (OOPSLA 2025, ACM 3763112; arXiv 2509.07609v1). Wei, Bračevac, Jia, Bao, Rompf, 'Polymorphic Reachability Types: Tracking Freshness, Aliasing, and Separation in Higher-Order Generic Programs' (POPL 2024, ACM 3632856). Jia, Liu, He, Rompf, 'Typestate via Revocable Capabilities' (Oct 2025). Crary, Walker, Morrisett (POPL 1999). Pottier & Protzenko, 'Mezzo' (TOPLAS 2013). Lean mechanization exists in System Capless artifact.
gaps.resolution: Resolved by §16.4 'Reach Modality on Container Kinds'. Surface syntax: 'class Container<rcap c, F: Type -> Type>' introduces the universally-quantified capability set c. Concept gates: 'where Capabilities(a) ⊑ c'. Subsumption: a container with broader capability c' is a subtype of one with narrower c when c ⊑ c'. Composes with linearity (dim 3): a Linear<Container<...>> on a container with rcap c containing only Linear capabilities is well-typed. Direct port from System Capless artifact.

gaps.name: ixmonad_foundation_tier_t
gaps.description: FX's Tier T (Protocol typestate) was previously treated as a bespoke transition relation specific to session types. Sessions, Machines, Transactions, Mutex blocks, STM blocks, and many other constructs are individual instances of the indexed monad (Atkey 2009): a monad parameterized by initial and final state. Without the unifying foundation, FX would require separate kernel rules for each instance.
gaps.prior_art: Atkey, 'Parameterised Notions of Computation' (JFP 2009). McBride, 'Kleisli Arrows of Outrageous Fortune' (2011). Petricek, Orchard, Mycroft, 'Coeffects' (ICFP 2014). Bonchi, Di Lavore, Román, 'Effectful Mealy Machines' (LICS 2025 / CALCO 2025). The LICS 2025 result shows their bisimilarity reduces to free-uniform-feedback equivalence — automatic at the IxMonad law level.
gaps.resolution: Resolved by §31 'IxMonad as Tier T Canonical Instance'. class IxMonad<F: (Idx, Idx, Type) -> Type> with ireturn and ibind plus three laws (left_id, right_id, assoc). Stdlib instances: Channel<P_in, P_out, T>, Machine<S_in, S_out, T>, Tx<S_in, S_out, T>, Mutex<L_in, L_out, T>. Tier T sessions become the canonical instance whose state set is the set of session types. Per-instance laws SMT-checked at instance declaration site.

gaps.name: profunctor_optics_stdlib
gaps.description: FX's stdlib previously lacked a unified algebra for views, lenses, prisms, traversals, folds, getters, setters, isomorphisms, affine traversals. Each 'view into a structure' pattern required hand-rolled templates. The profunctor-optics framework (Riley 2018, Pickering-Gibbons-Wu POPL 2018) gives a unified algebra: every such pattern is an Optic c s t a b for a profunctor constraint c. Composition by function composition; the meet of profunctor constraints handles per-instance composition.
gaps.prior_art: van Laarhoven 'CPS-based functional references' (2009). Pickering, Gibbons, Wu, 'Profunctor Optics: Modular Data Accessors' (Programming 2017, POPL 2018). Riley, 'Categories of Optics' (arXiv:1809.00738, 2018). Boisseau & Gibbons, 'What You Needa Know about Yoneda: Profunctor Optics and the Yoneda Lemma' (ICFP 2018). Clarke, Elkins, Gibbons, Loregian, Milewski, Pillmore, Román, 'Profunctor Optics, a Categorical Update' (arXiv:2001.07488).
gaps.resolution: Resolved by Std.Optics stdlib module. Optic type-class hierarchy: Lens (Strong), Prism (Choice), AffineTraversal (Strong + Choice), Iso (Profunctor), Traversal (Wander). Per-class laws verified at instance declaration. Composition by function composition; profunctor-representation theorem (Boisseau-Gibbons) gives the factorization (s -> r) × (r × b -> t).

gaps.name: capability_modality
gaps.description: FX has fractional permissions (§6.4) for the shared-borrow setting. The capability discipline of Mezzo / Capless / Reach extends this with named capability tags that flow through computation with the capability's identity preserved. Manual realizations (Permission<Tag>, SharedPermission<Tag>, Pool<Tag> in C++ template families) substitute for what the language should express directly.
gaps.prior_art: As gap #163 (System Capless line) plus Mezzo (Pottier-Protzenko TOPLAS 2013), Crary-Walker-Morrisett (POPL 1999), Iris (Jung et al. JFP 2018) for the higher-order ghost state foundation.
gaps.resolution: Resolved by §6.3 dim-cap modality classification at Tier L. Lattice: (P(NamedCaps), ⊆, ∪, ∅). Validity predicate: composition is valid iff intersected capabilities are shareable. Kernel rules: standard graded-modality intro/elim. Reach modality (gap #163) is rcap c — an instantiation with a fixed upper-bound capability set c. Surface syntax: cap(c) T for the capability-tagged type. Composition with Usage (dim 3): a linear value carrying a unique capability is the canonical ownership pattern.

gaps.name: region_calculus_primitive
gaps.description: FX has lifetime parameters (§8.1) but previously had no kernel-level region-block form. Tofte-Talpin 1997 formalizes 'letregion r in body' as a kernel construct: every allocation in body lives in r, and r's deallocation at scope exit is type-checked. Without the kernel form, region-allocated structures require ad-hoc lifetime threading at every allocation site.
gaps.prior_art: Tofte & Talpin, 'Region-based Memory Management' (Information and Computation 1997). Grossman et al., 'Region-based Memory Management in Cyclone' (PLDI 2002). Walker, Crary, Morrisett, 'Typed Memory Management via Static Capabilities' (TOPLAS 2000). Lattner & Adve, 'Automatic Pool Allocation' (PLDI 2005). Polonius next-generation borrow checker (Rust 2025h2 goal).
gaps.resolution: Resolved by §8 'Region Block Form'. Surface syntax: 'region r; ... end region;' block. Allocation strategy Alloc(Region(r)) becomes type-checked at the block boundary. Generalizes the existing §8.2 with_arena library function with a typed primitive. Kernel translation per Appendix H Σ + linearity discipline.

gaps.name: reagents_calculus_stdlib
gaps.description: FX previously had no algebraic foundation for composable lock-free concurrent data structures. Each lock-free primitive (SpscRing, MpscRing, MpmcRing, ChaseLevDeque, AtomicSnapshot) required a separate verification effort. Reagents (Turon PLDI 2012, ported to OCaml Multicore as kcas) give an algebraic calculus for composing them via reagent combinators (sequential >>>, choice <+>, conjunction <*>, atomic primitive upd). The composition is verified via Cosmo (concurrent separation logic for Multicore OCaml, POPL 2021).
gaps.prior_art: Turon, 'Reagents: Expressing and Composing Fine-Grained Concurrency' (PLDI 2012). kcas (OCaml Multicore ecosystem, supersedes Reagents in production). Cosmo: Concurrent Separation Logic for Multicore OCaml (POPL 2021). Saturn lock-free data structure library.
gaps.resolution: Resolved by Std.Concurrent.Reagent stdlib module with the reagent calculus. Reagent<a, b> is the type of a non-blocking transformation. Combinators: pure, >>>, <+>, <*>, upd, cas, swap. Each reagent execution is wrapped in an atomic transaction (k-CAS via the kcas library). Stdlib instances: 30+ atomic data structures as reagent compositions. Cosmo-style separation logic for verification (depends on Iris-grade BI, gap #169).

gaps.name: iris_bi_labeled_bunches
gaps.description: FX §6.4 declares 'separation logic IS the usage grade' — true for the affine fragment, but higher-order ghost state (step-indexed propositions, named invariants, monoid-valued ghosts) requires labeled BI per Iris. Without it, FX cannot encode non-trivial concurrent invariants. The 2025 Iris frontier (Nola, Lilo, Lawyer) makes the labeled BI usable for liveness as well as safety.
gaps.prior_art: O'Hearn & Pym, 'The Logic of Bunched Implications' (1999). Jung-Krebbers-Birkedal et al., 'Iris from the Ground Up' (JFP 2018). Liu et al., 'Quantified Underapproximation via Labeled Bunches' (OOPSLA 2025, ACM 3591275). Nola: Later-Free Ghost State (OOPSLA 2025, ACM 3729250). Lilo (Lee et al. OOPSLA 2025). Lawyer (POPL/OOPSLA 2026). Iris workshop 2025.
gaps.resolution: Resolved by FX/Kernel/Bi/ module with the BI algebraic core: emp, *, -*, named monoid composition, labeled bunches. Verified obligations discharged through the verify preset (Iris-style proof mode). Decidable-fragment SMT shortcut. Foundation for gap #168 (reagents) and the §11.18 liveness fragment. Lean mechanization commitment: Lean port of Iris's BI core (~5K lines).

gaps.name: vector_clocks_causal_histories_types
gaps.description: Distributed-coordination FX programs previously lacked a first-class vector_clock(participants) type and happens_before predicate. The replay-ordering refinement (gap #161) records vector clocks; without a kernel-level type, the happens-before checks are ad-hoc.
gaps.prior_art: Lamport, 'Time, Clocks, and the Ordering of Events' (CACM 1978). Mattern, 'Virtual Time and Global States of Distributed Systems' (1989). Fidge (1988). Kulkarni et al., 'Logical Physical Clocks (HLC)' (OPODIS 2014). Almeida et al., 'Interval Tree Clocks' (2008). Agarwal & Garg, 'Chain Clocks' (2005).
gaps.resolution: Resolved by Std.Time.Causal stdlib module with vc<n: nat> (vector clock), hlc (hybrid logical clock), happens_before<n>(a: vc(n), b: vc(n)) : bool, and related operations. The happens_before predicate is decidable; decide discharges it in the §10.4 tactic framework. Kernel-level recognition that vc<n> participates in dim 7 Lifetime (causal ordering refines reference validity scope).

gaps.name: crdt_class_hierarchy
gaps.description: FX's @[structure] class with CommMonoid is the right algebraic foundation but does not extend to lattice + CRDT-style discipline. CRDT convergence is a property of join-semilattice structure with monotonic updates. BloomL (Conway et al. SoCC 2012) extends Bloom with lattice types and CALM analysis; Propel (Programming 2023) type-checks CRDT convergence; Katara (PLDI 2025) synthesizes CRDTs from sequential specs.
gaps.prior_art: Shapiro, Preguiça, Baquero, Zawirski, 'Conflict-Free Replicated Data Types' (SSS 2011). Conway, Marczak, Gowda, Liu, Hellerstein, 'Logic and Lattices for Distributed Programming' (BloomL, SoCC 2012). Kuper & Newton, 'LVars' (FHPC 2013). Hellerstein & Alvaro, 'Keeping CALM' (arXiv:1901.01930). Type-Checking CRDT Convergence (Propel, Programming 2023). Katara: Synthesizing CRDTs with Verified Lifting (PLDI 2025). Bolt-On Strong Consistency (OOPSLA 2025).
gaps.resolution: Resolved by Std.Distributed.CRDT stdlib module with the CRDT class hierarchy: Lattice<T> (commutative + associative + idempotent join), CvRDT<T> (state-based CRDT), MonotonicCounter<T> (with monotonic increment), LWWRegister<T>, ORSet<T>, PNCounter<T>. Each instance's laws SMT-checked at declaration site. Composition: products of CRDTs, maps to CRDTs, sequential prefixes (BloomL prefix lattice). Composes with Bridge modality for free convergence proofs.

gaps.name: faceted_values
gaps.description: FX §12 enforces noninterference via security lattice walks. For deployment where strict noninterference produces too many spurious violations (Quan dissertation 2024 reports 90% reduction with relaxed semantics), the faceted-values approach (Austin-Flanagan POPL 2012) is the type-theoretic mechanism: a value carries both public and secret views, and operations propagate both in parallel. Combined with the Bridge modality, noninterference for the faceted fragment becomes a parametricity theorem.
gaps.prior_art: Austin & Flanagan, 'Faceted Values for Untrusted Information Flow Security' (POPL 2012). Schmitz, Algehed, Flanagan, Hritcu, 'Faceted Dynamic Information Flow via Control and Data Monads' (POST 2018). Algehed & Bernardy, 'Simple Noninterference from Parametricity' (ICFP 2019). QTFlow (arXiv:2401.17819, 2024). Quan dissertation (Duke ECOOP 2024).
gaps.resolution: Resolved by §12 'Faceted Values'. Faceted<a> stdlib type with make_facet, observe, declassify_facet primitives. Per-policy declassification: faceted-value declassification operates per-policy, not per-value. Combined with the Bridge modality: noninterference for polymorphic faceted-value operations becomes a free theorem provable via parametricity.

gaps.name: decidable_decide_framework
gaps.description: FX defers refinement obligations to SMT. For the decidable fragment (bit-vector equalities, finite-set membership, structural properties), proof-by-reflection is faster, more reliable, and produces certificates SMT cannot. Lean 4's Decidable α typeclass + decide tactic + native_decide make constructive decidability first-class. FX adopts the same.
gaps.prior_art: Geuvers, Wiedijk, Zwanenburg, 'A constructive proof of the Fundamental Theorem of Algebra without using the rationals' (TYPES 2002). Boutin, 'Using Reflection to Build Efficient and Certified Decision Procedures' (TACS 1997). de Moura & Ullrich, 'The Lean 4 Theorem Prover and Programming Language' (CADE 2021). Aesop (Lean 4 community).
gaps.resolution: Resolved by Std.Logic with class Decidable<P: prop> and decide tactic. Stdlib instances for primitives (Eq, Ord, finite sets). The decide tactic discharges goals via constructive decidability when possible; the existing SMT periphery handles the rest. Native compilation via native_decide for performance-critical paths. Removes load on the SMT periphery; provides constructive certificates that SMT does not.

gaps.name: coeffects_graded_comonad
gaps.description: Effects (FX dim 4) describe what a computation does; coeffects describe what a computation needs from its context — environment, cache, capabilities, fuel. FX previously had no formal coeffect mechanism. Petricek-Orchard-Mycroft ICFP 2014 gives coeffects as the dual of effects via comonads; Choudhury-Krishnaswami ICFP 2020 gives the graded version.
gaps.prior_art: Petricek, Orchard, Mycroft, 'Coeffects: Unified Static Analysis of Context-Dependence' (ICFP 2014). Choudhury & Krishnaswami, 'Coeffects via Resource Annotations' (ICFP 2020). Gaboardi, Katsumata, Orchard et al., 'Combining Effects and Coeffects via Grading' (ICFP 2016).
gaps.resolution: Resolved by §6 reclassification. Dim 7 Lifetime, dim 12 Clock, dim 19 Reentrancy treated as coeffects (context requirements) rather than effects (operations performed). Formally graded comonadic annotations: a function's coeffect is what its context must provide. Surface annotation via existing 'with' (no new syntax); the typing rule for coeffect propagation is dual to effect propagation. Formalized in mode theory as adjoint modalities to the effect modalities.

gaps.name: sail_integration_hardware
gaps.description: fx_design.md §20.7 (ISA Formalization) treats the per-arch ISA as an FX module. The de facto standard is Sail (Armstrong et al. POPL 2019), the official executable golden model of RISC-V International. Sail supports Isabelle, Rocq, and Lean for theorem-prover backends. FX consumes Sail directly rather than redefine ISA semantics.
gaps.prior_art: Armstrong et al., 'ISA Semantics for ARMv8-A, RISC-V, and CHERI-MIPS' (POPL 2019). CHERIoT-Ibex Comprehensive Formal Verification (arXiv:2502.04738, Feb 2025). Morello-Cerise (Hammond et al. PACMPL June 2025). Zaliva et al., 'Formal Mechanised Semantics of CHERI C' (ASPLOS 2024). A CHERI C Memory Model for Verified Temporal Safety (CPP 2025). Sail RISC-V github.com/riscv/sail-riscv.
gaps.resolution: Resolved by §18 / §20.7 'Sail Integration'. Sail importer in fxc compiler reads Sail modules and exposes their semantics as FX Hardware-mode types. Surface attribute: @[from_sail("riscv")] on a hardware module imports the Sail spec. Verified-RTL refinement via the refinement declaration with the Sail spec as the spec side. The CHERIoT-Ibex methodology is the proof template. No new FX spec language needed.

gaps.name: wit_lowering_contracts
gaps.description: fx_design.md §14 contracts have abstract format declarations. Wire mode commits to a serialize ⊣ parse adjunction. The concrete operational target is WebAssembly Interface Types (WIT) — the language-agnostic IDL of the WebAssembly Component Model. WIT-typed FX modules interoperate with Rust, Go, JavaScript, Python on equal footing through the Component Model's canonical ABI.
gaps.prior_art: WebAssembly Component Model (component-model.bytecodealliance.org). WIT spec (github.com/WebAssembly/component-model/blob/main/design/mvp/WIT.md). WASI 0.2.0 (Jan 2024) and WASI 0.3 (H1 2025). Wasmtime; wit-bindgen.
gaps.resolution: Resolved by §14 'WIT Lowering for Contracts'. format wit; clause on contract declarations. The fxc compiler emits a WIT specification per contract; cross-language consumers generate bindings via wit-bindgen. WASI 0.3 native async makes FX's Async effect a peer of WASI's async type. Round-trip law witnessed by contract validators per the existing §14.5 generated operations.

gaps.name: smoothe_egraph_extraction
gaps.description: Forge optimization (§21) commits to e-graph-based rewriting. Traditional ILP-based extraction is limited to linear cost functions; SmoothE (Cai-Yang-Deng-Zhang ASPLOS 2025 Best Paper) replaces ILP with continuous probabilistic optimization on GPUs, supporting non-linear cost functions (predicted_cycles + α·ULP_drift + β·binary_size). 8-37× speedup over ILP.
gaps.prior_art: SmoothE (ASPLOS 2025 Best Paper, ACM 3669940.3707262). egg (Willsey et al.). Felix (ASPLOS 2024). MCTS-based extraction (PACT 2024). LGuess (EGRAPHS 2025). BoolE (DAC 2025). Mind the Abstraction Gap (OOPSLA 2025). Contextual Equality Saturation (SAS 2025).
gaps.resolution: Resolved by §21 adoption of SmoothE as the e-graph extraction backend for fxc Phase C optimizer. GPU acceleration optional (CPU-bound fallback for small e-graphs). Non-linear cost function support unlocks per-vendor cost models with cross-vendor ULP drift and binary size penalties.
